channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Lies of P Sales Hit 4m as Sequel Info Remains Elusive
item.content:encoded:
Developer and publisher Neowiz has announced that its Soulslike Lies of P has passed another major sales milestone, although unfortunately, we still don't really know what's happening with the long-awaited sequel.

In a post on X, Neowiz confirms that the game has hit four million sales around the world, a figure that appears to include both physical and digital sales.

The new sales figure means that Lies of P has racked up another million sales since June last year, when it managed to hit three million following the release of the Overture DLC (which was previously intended for the first quarter of 2025).

With news of Lies of P's latest sales milestone comes speculation regarding what's happening with the long-awaited sequel, which was first revealed back in late 2023 alongside the first tease of what would eventually become Overture.

At that point, Lies of P director Ji Won Choi said that the game's development team was focused on "developing the DLC and working on our sequel", which was followed by two years of near-silence regarding the project.

In January, Neowiz co-CEO Sean Kim told Game Informer that Round8, the internal Neowiz studio behind Lies of P, is "excited" about the work it's doing on the game and that the team is "fully focused on development". That's all we got, though.

I'm hoping that we hear something more concrete from Neowiz and Round8 soon, especially given that a project being fairly deep into development is no longer any guarantee that it will ever see the light of day.

If you're not familiar with Lies of P, it's a Soulslike that serves as a loose reimagining of Carlo Collodi's classic children's novel The Adventures of Pinocchio. The emphasis there is on "loose", though; I don't think you'll get much from this if you want a faithful interpretation of the source material.

Lies of P is available to check out right now on PC, PlayStation, and Xbox, and you can read my review of the game right here.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/lies-of-p-sales-4m
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Experience Call of Cthulhu Live On Glass Cannon Network's Nationwide Tour - Troy Lavallee Interview
item.content:encoded:
Glass Cannon Network, who you might know from their variety of TTRPG actual play shows, including The Glass Cannon podcast, New Game Who Dis?, Time for Chaos, and more, has announced that this year they'll be touring the nation playing Call of Cthulhu live! Ahead of their tour, we got the chance to sit down and talk with Troy Lavallee – GCN Ceo/Founder and GM for the live shows – to discuss why Call of Cthulhu is perfect for a live show.

Glass Cannon Network Found A Non-D&D Niche To Fill

To catch you up with who Troy Lavallee and the Glass Cannon Network is they started as a podcast playing Pathfinder. "We started in 2015, just a couple of buddies and I sitting in a sweaty Queens apartment, decided 'what if we started recording our home game?'"

Back then, Actual Plays weren't much of a thing; major AP Critical Role had only just started in March of the same year, but it wasn't nearly as crowded a market as it is today. "You could count on one hand the people making actual play podcasts. We came in at a really good time and it grew from there, and as it continued to grow, we started exploring other games and other genres, and then branched out into live show performances and appearances and now we have a whole network of shows and personalities."

While Glass Cannon Podcast began in Pathfinder, they immediately recognized that they were filling an even smaller niche in the Actual Play space by not covering Dungeons & Dragons. "We started in Pathfinder 1st Ed, I played AD&D 2nd Ed, and then I didn't play for 20-something years until my buddy got me back into it. I asked What do you do, play D&D?' and he explained 'No, actually, we play the spiritual successor to D&D' and I had never played Pathfinder so we just started playing and it felt like the crunchy granular D&D that I played growing up, so when we started making a podcast, we naturally made a Pathfinder podcast."

"Now, as more and more shows came out, everyone else was making D&D podcasts, so we filled an interesting niche for people who only play Pathfinder, and so as we continue to grow, I saw an opportunity here. 'Why don't we make that kind of our thing, we just make shows of all these other games."

Rattling off a list of games that the Glass Cannon Network has covered, Lavallee mentioned Delta Green, Call of Cthulhu, Traveller, and Starfinder among them. "Then we have a show called New Game Who Dis? where every three weeks we play a new game. Indie RPGs, stuff that has been around for years like GURPS, that no one really made a show of… You name it, we've made a show of it."

Variety Is The Spice Of TTRPGs

Interestingly, Lavallee also brought up how, by playing so many different games, they were able to use it as an extensive bank of mechanics that can be adapted and brought into other systems.

"After a while, every system you play becomes a hybrid of your favorite systems. We do it intentionally now. We'll be playing one game and be like 'you know what, let's do a Blades In The Dark mechanic here because it feels like we need to have a flashback scene. Eventually, everyone's just going to be playing their own home version of some game and straying away from the action rules."

Taking the podcast on tour, previously Glass Cannon Network had toured with Pathfinder, but "a few years ago at GenCon we started doing two live shows outside of the Con. I said "Instead of doing Pathfinder, let's do Call of Cthulhu' because we had a massive Nyarlathotep series called Time For Chaos that was doing really well and I felt like Call of Cthulhu lent itself to a very interesting night."

"The whole time I was thinking it would be great if we could pivot the whole tour to this"

Lavallee praised the Call of Cthulhu system for how well it adapts to a live setting. "You never know where it's going to go." Some key features were its unpredictability and chaos allowing it to fit an almost 'episodic' format to be contained in a single live show.

The tour starts next week in Los Angeles, CA, and after a few shows there, the tour is hitting the road, travelling to a wide variety of states, including Oregon, Texas, Louisiana, Georgia, Wisconsin, and many more. As well as touring different cities, the cast will also be rotating as the show goes on.

"This year, I believed these need to all be one-shot/standalone scenarios, and what better way to get everyone involved with our network on the road than to have a different cast at every single show. Not only is the genre of horror going to be different every show, the investigators will be different, the players on stage will be different, and everything will have a unique flow in the sense that some shows might start right away with something horrible happening and the characters have to deal with it, people could die within the first 10 minutes of the show. Whereas in other live shows, it may not be until the end of the show that the shit hits the fan and everyone starts running for their lives."

The goal of this is that everything should feel like a completely unique experience

Lavallee isn't alone in the creation of each of the one-shots; through a partnership with Chaosium (Call of Cthulhu, RuneQuest) Lavallee has been collaborating on these one-shots to make sure they're the best they could be ahead of a live performance.

"I sent a one-sheet that goes out to all of their writers with what I'm looking for and how it's all stand-alone scenarios, but there might be some loosely connected overarching plot that only I really know, and if you watch every episode, you might see some strings starting to connect. But you also don't need to see every show, you can come in with no knowledge of the previous show and walk out and never see one again and have a wonderful experience."

"It's been interesting [..] I just have the first three scenarios, then I get the next batch of six sometime in the next couple of weeks so I can start to prepare for Chicago or Salem. I took those first three scenarios, and I'm like 'Ok, which one do I want to do night one and why? And what would be a good night two show and is there a way that I'm thinking that these could be connected?'"

Discussing the placement of each show, Lavallee highlighted some of the factors that go into the decision, such as overarching narrative, what players and characters will be at that show, and where the show will take place, capping it off by explaining that it's been a unique experience "that even I am surprised as the process rolls along. There's a world where even I won't know what's happening until I'm up on stage."

How Glass Cannon Network Kicks Off A One-Shot

Knowing how long the group has been playing together, I was interested in a bit of the behind the scenes planning that goes into starting a successful one-shot, and what GM tools Lavallee keeps close.

"It's funny, if this were a film, something would happen in the first 10 minutes. There would be the inciting event and that's what hooks people," Lavallee began talking on how to best draw the audience in. "With our shows, we're trying to really emulate the experience of what it's like to sit around with your best friends and play a role-playing game. What do you do when you sit around with your friends? You just kinda bullshit and goof around with each other."

"We lean into that, and that's become a hallmark of what we do. Sometimes we'll banter for 10-15 minutes about just nonsense." Lavallee compared the experience to going to see a theater or a stand-up gig where there's always an opener.

"What it does is it gets both the players warmed up and the audience warmed up as well so we're all goofing off, leaning into whatever topic we end up into and then we get into the show and there's a very sharp turn where all of a sudden you realize that part of the show is over and now we're getting into the story. Both the players and the audience are ready for the horrors that are about to unfold."

As to knowing what was behind Lavallee's GM screen, he was quick to answer, "Vodka, a nice vodka," he laughed.

Expanding further on tools more directly related to running TTRPGs, he continued, "Running games, I've really stripped down what I've used behind the screen in home games, studio games, and definitely on tour. My idea setup is to walk up there and not even have a Keeper Screen. I like to have my laptop, I like to have a tray with dice… and a nice cocktail."

"I used to have three iPads with all of the monster stats and different initiative boards, now I just keep it simple. By doing that it lets me focus on listening to the players because I can't truly react to them if I'm worried about all my knick-knacks behind the screen. I really just want to be a player as much as they are. A lot of people forget that the Game Master, the Keeper, is a player too."

"If you can put all that aside and engage with the game you can be just as happily surprised by what happens as the players can."

Call of Cthulhu Live! kicks off next week in LA at the Dynasty Typewriter and will be touring around the nation all the way through to December so if you're interested in watching a horror adventure live, be sure to check out their website for information on tour dates, locations, and ticket prices.

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/features/experience-call-of-cthulhu-live-on-glass-cannon-networks-nationwide-tour-troy
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Counter-Strike 2 Reload Rework Casually Changes the Entire Game
item.content:encoded:
A new update for Counter-Strike 2 has been released, and while it might sound like there's not much to chew on, it's actually a pretty big patch that seems like it'll change the game completely.

Over on Steam, Valve says the update is designed to give Counter-Strike 2 players "plenty to think about", and its first big change will definitely do that, especially if you're a long-time devotee.

Before the update, reloading guns in Counter-Strike 2 would basically put leftover ammo "into an essentially endless reserve supply", as Valve says, but with the new update, reloading will discard all remaining ammo in your magazine. The change should add "significant trade-offs" to the act of reloading, Valve believes.

Now, reloading your gun will take a fresh magazine from your reserves whenever you do it, and each weapon will have a certain number of reserve magazines. Some will have fewer "to reward efficiency and precision", while others will have more "to encourage spamming through walls and smokes".

The reloading rework is the biggest change that's been introduced as part of this update, but it's not the only one. You can now use map guides for the first five rounds of each half, which should help you to get the lay of the land.

Additionally, it's now possible to set up custom games that you and your friends can take part in, so if you don't feel like Counter-Strike 2's usual high-energy sweaty shooting, then you can try something a little more (or less!) relaxed.

If you've been away for a while and you're wondering what Counter-Strike 2 is, it's essentially the relaunched and reworked version of Counter-Strike: Global Offensive, with Valve surprise-launching the game's fresh coat of paint in 2023.

Since then, the game has climbed in popularity terms, with soaring player counts and huge revenue numbers demonstrating that it's still very much top dog in the shooter space.

Of course, whether that remains true after this latest update is another matter. You can check the game out right now on PC via Steam, where you can play for free.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/counter-strike-2-reload-rework-update
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
